replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: What issue about Sweden surprised you the most?

comments.author: @KerwynWilson-w3q
comments.text: In my country we have an increase of violent crime, it's considered high. When we are chilling on the beach or by the river side you'll find many kids and adults napping outside. We also have children napping in the parks or savannas which we use for sporting activities. We don't leave doors unlocked unless there's at least 1 resident in the home but most houses are built and surrounded by fences or concrete walls separating each dwelling from the other on 3 sides and the road on the fourth side. This way, in every home, it's possible to nap outdoors without leaving the premises and homes may be built with galleries or patios for  outdoor napping. However, we will never allow children to be unattended or without supervision and that is the law here. You must have direct sight of the child or be in earshot of children in your care since if something happens to the child and you weren't able to prevent it but are present it's considered accidental, however if something happens and you're not in range of the child, you would be charged with child negligence and leaving a child unsupervised. Until the child is 18 years old by law, they can't be left anywhere including their own homes without adult supervision. So we nap outside just in a different way.

replies.author: @KerwynWilson-w3q
replies.text: ​@AmazingGlobalDiscoverieskeep doing it. Very well produced and presented. Looking forward to seeing more of these videos and in their own way, the videos close that cultural gaps between nations by the break down of the various lifestyles in the world. Thanks for that.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: I really like the way you explained that in your country, children may nap outside, but always with supervision and within a protected home or community space. That balance between fresh air, family life, and responsibility makes a lot of sense. Sweden’s practice can seem surprising from the outside, but your comment reminds us that every country has its own way of handling trust, safety, and child care. I appreciate you adding this perspective.

comments.author: @correctednews1463
comments.text:
People who post their location are being scammed.

First, YouTube pays based on number of views, length of views and number of comments. 
This new FLOOD of AI trash channels could care less where you are from, they just want the money.

Also, when they collect this kind of data, they can then sell that location data to 'interested parties'.

It is bad enough that they grab your IP address (identify  your computer on the internet), now they know where you are.

Don't be stupid. Protect your privacy.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text:
You are right that online privacy is very important, and people should always be careful about what personal information they share publicly.
Just to be clear, we do not ask viewers to post exact addresses or any private details. If someone chooses to comment, a general country or region is already more than enough, and even that is completely optional. We truly appreciate viewers joining the discussion, but protecting privacy should always come first.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: Yes, the national language is Swedish, but English is very widely used in Sweden, especially in cities, tourist areas, public transport, shops, and online services. Many Swedes speak excellent English, so visitors often notice a lot of English signage too. It’s one of the things that makes Sweden feel very easy to explore for foreigners.

comments.author: @ckzf1842
comments.text: The only European nation where I have experienced rude and condescending racism while staying at their top hotel in Stockholm ! Will NEVER go there again !!

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: I’m really sorry to hear that you had such an unpleasant experience in Stockholm. No traveler should feel looked down on or treated unfairly, especially when visiting another country with an open heart. Sweden has many beautiful sides, but experiences like yours are also important to hear because they remind us that no place is perfect. Thank you for sharing this honestly. I hope your future travels bring you much warmer and more respectful memories.

comments.author: @sasharai559
comments.text: I have lived in Sweden for more than sixty years and came to Sweden for a swedish girl. This is a wonderful country in spite of some of the diffculties like taxes and the dark winters. The taxes I paid while working, and complained about, paid for two major operations and a month in the hospital. My taxes paid for my education upto university level so I stopped complaining a long time ago. The light summer nights and the forests and lovely lakes lift my spirits every year. If you live in a country where the social life is more easy going then think twice about moving to Sweden because making friends is very hard. Even the swedes find it diffcult and sadly there are a lot of lonely people out there. If you are an introvert then welcome but if you are an extrovert then heaven help you.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: Thank you so much for sharing this beautiful and honest perspective. Hearing from someone who has lived in Sweden for more than sixty years adds so much depth to the discussion. Your point about loneliness and making friends is very important too. Sweden can be a wonderful country, but the social culture may feel difficult for people who are used to a more open and easygoing social life. That is something many outsiders do not fully understand before moving there.

comments.author: @richardsetters8547
comments.text: Oh yes

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: Thank you, my friend! Glad you’re watching and enjoying the journey with us. Sweden really has so many surprising sides, doesn’t it? What part of Swedish life impressed you the most?

comments.author: @HarlandJamesNelson
comments.text: I am a second geration American with a strong Swedish heritage. All 4 of my grandparents emigrated to the US from Sweden. The conception that Swedes all are blond haired and blue eyed is a Hollywood creation. As this video mentions most Swedes are dark haired. My father told me he had blond hair as a child but it turned black overnight when he was 18 years old. My mother also had dark hair.  But then genetics are funny. I am 75 years old and still have blond hair and blue eyes.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: Your family history makes this topic feel even more real. I completely agree with you — the idea that all Swedes are blond and blue-eyed is definitely more of a Hollywood image than reality. Genetics really can surprise us, especially across generations. It’s wonderful that at seventy-five, you still carry those blond hair and blue eyes from your Swedish roots.

comments.author: @leswallace2426
comments.text: Burning trash for energy ISN'T recycling, in fact investment in waste to energy plants means they require constant streams of 'waste'. This undermines reduce, reuse, recycle efforts and their superior environmental and social benefits. Plus lots of trees, but due to their rapacious logging industry very, very little old growth forest left.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: You make a very fair point, and I appreciate you adding this perspective. Waste-to-energy is often presented as a clean solution, but it is definitely not the same as reducing, reusing, or true recycling. And you’re right that Sweden’s forestry situation is more complicated than the “green paradise” image many people imagine.

comments.author: @georgeolson3996
comments.text: "Sveryah"  -- Dalsland  --  Stora Le - Hogen farm summer 1961 I was eleven, an only child and I met a fourteen  of at least two  dozen cousins .  A girl cousin on the island of Tjorn stunned me with her sparkling personality and  simultaneously her beauty of form and vivacity that I was smitten and that sadly , frustratingly we were cousins and she was beyond my reach in energy and in my way of logic and also by reason and science we were too closely related to marry and have children. Another cousin Lisa who was 21 that i met in Stockholm was gentle and beautiful in a quite way. More than 50 years later when she came to visit in Seattle  I did not recognize her but she managed to identify me.  Then suddenly it snapped into place for my father's older cousins had told me that my grandmother's younger sister was a beautiful when she was a young woman and  Lisa at 70 was suddenly known to my mind,,,

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text:
What a beautiful memory you shared. It sounds like Sweden, Dalsland, Tjörn, and those family connections left a very deep mark on your heart. Sometimes childhood memories stay with us in such a powerful way, especially when they are tied to people who seemed full of life, beauty, and kindness.

The part about Lisa recognizing you after so many years is truly touching. Family resemblance, memory, and emotion can come back in one sudden moment, just like you described. Thank you for sharing such a personal piece of your life with us.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: Sweden is definitely not a perfect country, and high taxes, housing problems, and pressure on the middle class are real issues many people talk about. In this video, I wanted to show both sides — the beauty, the comfort, but also the hidden problems behind that image. Every system has strengths and weaknesses.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: Interestingly, when looking at the latest data from organizations like the World Health Organization, Sweden's actual rankings are much closer to the global average today than the old myths suggest, but the underlying challenge of winter darkness and quiet solitude remains very real. It really makes you think about the delicate balance between external societal stability and internal peace of mind.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: You are right, Sweden has given the world some unforgettable names like ABBA, Volvo, and Ericsson, and those legacies still say a lot about Swedish creativity and discipline. I also agree with your point that comfort can sometimes make talented people less visible to the world. But Sweden still has so much innovation, design, music, and technology quietly growing beneath the surface. Maybe the next great Swedish name will not look like the old ones, but it may carry the same spirit in a new form.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: Sweden has changed a lot over the years, and like many European countries, it is now much more diverse than it was in older footage. In the video, we tried to show both the beautiful cultural side and some of the newer challenges Sweden is facing today.

comments.author: @Paul-r8w4x
comments.text: The Good Old Days

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: Sweden has changed a lot over time, but many traditions and memories still make people feel nostalgic. Thank you for watching and sharing that thought, my friend.

replies.author: @TravelToFacts
replies.text: There are images that truly evoke memories of a simpler and more peaceful time ❤ but I wonder if The Good Old Days really existed… or if they're just more beautiful in our memories?

replies.author: @ljprep6250
replies.text: Exactly.  None of Sweden's streets look like that today.  How many cities are entire NO GO zones, where police aren't safe to patrol?  Show us today's Malmo, eh?

comments.author: @tiredlawdog
comments.text:
I didn't see one fat or overweight person. I'm living in San Antonio, just about everyone here is overweight.
Lots of "all you eat places", and we do.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: San Antonio sounds like it has a very different food culture, especially with those “all you can eat” places. Food habits really say a lot about daily life in each country, don’t they? Thanks for sharing your perspective.

comments.author: @lenwiener2961
comments.text: The recycling program is IMPRESSIVE  !!

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: Yes, Sweden really does recycling on another level! It’s impressive how much responsibility people take in daily life, from sorting waste to returning bottles and cans. Small habits like that say a lot about the culture.
